commands.review_summary:
The branch increases registry artifact backup retry cadence and batch size, includes repairable exhausted jobs, repairs index-only backup gaps, parallelizes retry roots with byte caps, adds a Convex index, and expands tests.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence runtime backlog reproduction was run. Source inspection of current main clearly shows the throughput limit: 30-minute cadence, 25-job default batch, and pending-only due-job selection.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is needed; maintainer review should decide whether the production retry throughput envelope is acceptable for this collaborator PR.

target.title: Fix registry backup retry drain throughput
target.body:
## Summary
- Increase registry artifact retry cron cadence to every 5 minutes and drain up to 200 jobs per pass.
- Include repairable exhausted jobs, repair existing R2 indexes without reuploading already-backed-up artifacts, and retry index writes with bounded backoff.
- Process retry roots in parallel while keeping the same skill/package root sequential and capping full-artifact concurrency so max-size package uploads run one at a time.
- Add an indexed exhausted-job repair query so maxed-out exhausted rows cannot starve still-repairable jobs.

## Validation
- `PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bunx vitest run convex/registryArtifactBackups.test.ts convex/lib/registryArtifactBackup.test.ts convex/crons.test.ts`
- `PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bun run ci:static`
- `PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" VITE_CONVEX_URL=https://example.invalid bun run ci:unit`
- `PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bun run ci:types-build`
- `git diff --check`
- `PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH" bunx convex codegen`

## Review Notes
- Autoreview found and we fixed exhausted-job starvation and full-artifact memory budget issues.
- A final autoreview rerun recursed into another autoreview process instead of producing a clean verdict, so it was stopped after the accepted findings were addressed and the full local gates passed.
target.author: patrick-erichsen

commands.author: jesse-merhi

target.title: fix: restrict docs auth return origins to dev only
target.body:
## What this fixes

The ClawHub docs auth broker (`/auth/docs`, Ask Molty) signs a user in and then **auto-POSTs their ClawHub auth token** to the `return_to` origin's callback. The allowlist trusted loopback origins unconditionally:

```ts
const allowedDocsOrigins = new Set([
  "https://documentation.openclaw.ai",
  "https://docs.openclaw.ai",
  "http://localhost:4173",   // trusted in prod too
  "http://127.0.0.1:4173",
]);
```

In production, a user lured to `/auth/docs?return_to=http://localhost:4173/...` has their token POSTed to that loopback address. On a shared/multi-process machine, any local process bound to that port can capture a signed-in ClawHub bearer token. This is the `/auth/docs` half of **GHSA-mp85-24m3-52jg**.

## The fix: couple loopback trust to a local app origin

Instead of gating on a runtime env flag (which a staging/preview/misconfigured deployment could mis-set), the allowlist now permits a loopback `return_to` **only when the app itself is served from a loopback origin**:

```ts
function isAllowedDocsOrigin(origin: string, currentOrigin?: string | null) {
  if (productionDocsOrigins.has(origin)) return true;
  return isLocalDocsOrigin(origin) && isLocalDocsOrigin(currentOrigin ?? "");
}
```

- Production docs origins (`https://clawhub.ai`, `https://documentation.openclaw.ai`, `https://docs.openclaw.ai`) are always allowed.
- A loopback `return_to` is allowed only when `currentOrigin` is also loopback. A public deployment's origin is never loopback, so it can never POST the token to localhost — independent of `NODE_ENV` or build mode.
- On server render `currentOrigin` is unknown (`null`), so loopback is rejected there too; the auto-submit happens client-side where `window.location.origin` is known.

This restores the reviewed origin-coupled implementation that briefly existed on main (commit `e86aa30a`) before the docs-subdomain move reverted it. It also re-adds `https://clawhub.ai` as a production docs origin.

## CSP backstop

`vercel.json` `form-action` drops the loopback entries, so even if the application allowlist ever regressed, the production browser would block a cross-origin form POST to localhost:

```
form-action 'self' https://documentation.openclaw.ai https://docs.openclaw.ai
```

`'self'` covers `https://clawhub.ai`. The application allowlist and the CSP are now two independent layers.

## Invariant recorded

`specs/auth-identity.md` documents the docs-auth token-destination invariant and the requirement that the CSP `form-action` stay aligned with the application allowlist ([P2] from review).

## Why origin-coupling over a runtime env gate

An earlier revision of this PR gated loopback behind `isDevRuntime()`. Review (clawsweeper) correctly flagged that `isDevRuntime()` treats *any* non-`production` `NODE_ENV` as dev, so a public non-production runtime could fail open. Origin-coupling removes that dependency entirely: the trust is tied to where the app is actually running, not to an env signal.

## Verification

- `vitest run src/lib/docsAuth.test.ts src/routes/auth/-docs.test.tsx` — 9 passed. Covers: prod origins (incl. `clawhub.ai`) allowed; loopback rejected from a production app origin; loopback rejected when the app origin is unknown (server render); loopback allowed only when the app origin is loopback (both `localhost` and `127.0.0.1`); the route posts the token only to the prod docs callback.
- `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json` — exit 0.
- `oxlint --type-aware` and `oxfmt --check` — clean.

Rebased onto current `main`; branch is a single commit.


## Runtime proof (real browser)

Captured with Playwright driving the **real shipped `src/lib/docsAuth.ts`** (served by a bare Vite that transpiles the actual module — no copy, no mock) loaded at two real browser origins. `normalizeDocsReturnTo` reads the live `window.location.origin`, so each page load exercises the real origin-coupling decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[return_to origin] --> B{production docs origin?}
  B -- yes --> OK[ALLOW: clawhub.ai / docs.openclaw.ai / documentation.openclaw.ai]
  B -- no --> C{return_to is loopback?}
  C -- no --> NO[REJECT]
  C -- yes --> D{app origin is loopback?}
  D -- yes --> OKL[ALLOW local-to-local]
  D -- no --> NO2[REJECT: public app cannot return token to localhost]
```

**App origin `http://127.0.2.2:5199` (non-loopback hostname → prod-style):**

| return_to | verdict |
| --- | --- |
| `http://localhost:4173/start` | **REJECTED** |
| `http://127.0.0.1:4173/start` | **REJECTED** |
| `https://docs.openclaw.ai/install` | ALLOWED |
| `https://clawhub.ai/docs/auth` | ALLOWED |
| `https://evil.example.com/steal` | REJECTED |

**App origin `http://127.0.0.1:5199` (loopback → local dev):**

| return_to | verdict |
| --- | --- |
| `http://localhost:4173/start` | ALLOWED |
| `http://127.0.0.1:4173/start` | ALLOWED |
| `https://docs.openclaw.ai/install` | ALLOWED |
| `https://clawhub.ai/docs/auth` | ALLOWED |
| `https://evil.example.com/steal` | REJECTED |

The only difference between the two runs is the app's own origin: at a non-loopback (prod-style) origin both loopback `return_to` values are rejected, so the token-carrying form is never built for a localhost destination; at a loopback origin local-to-local docs auth still works.

**Scope of this proof:** it exercises the shipped origin-check module in a real browser at real origins. It is not the full authenticated `/auth/docs` POST — the local app SSR needs a Convex backend that isn't available here — but the origin check is the gate that decides whether the token form renders at all, so it is the security-relevant decision. Screenshots of both runs were captured locally (`docs-auth-prodstyle-origin.png`, `docs-auth-local-origin.png`).

## How to test

```text
# real-browser check of the shipped origin-coupling module
bun install
# serve src/lib/docsAuth.ts via a bare vite (no SSR), open the page at:
#   http://127.0.0.1:5199/...   -> loopback app origin (local dev)
#   http://127.0.2.2:5199/...   -> non-loopback app origin (prod-style)
# normalizeDocsReturnTo(returnTo) reads window.location.origin and rejects
# loopback return_to unless the app origin is itself loopback.

# unit + route coverage of the same contract
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/lib/docsAuth.test.ts src/routes/auth/-docs.test.tsx
```
target.author: jesse-merhi
